enrichment.summary: Kyle discusses the Voynich manuscript in response to a viewer request, arguing it is not a true mystery but simply an extinct language — most likely Etruscan — preserved through successive copies by a community in northern Italy. He uses this as a springboard to explore how languages die when empires spread, drawing on Roman history, the Gallic sack of Rome, and examples ranging from the Phoenician alphabet to Korean Hangul.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Not a mystery — it is simply an extinct language, almost certainly Etruscan
Languages die naturally when a dominant empire spreads and displaces local tongues, just as Latin displaced Etruscan and other Italic languages
More people and groups had written languages in antiquity than modern scholars give credit for, including the Scythians among their elite
Our ancient history is extremely thin because conquerors like the Gauls destroyed records and empires rarely documented the cultures they absorbed
The Etruscans were a distinct race with a completely unrelated language who lived right alongside early Rome

entities: Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire
